items.q: In Pythagoras' theorem, which side does c^2 represent?
items.correct: The hypotenuse — the longest side, opposite the right angle.
items.misconception: c^2 represents any side of the triangle as long as the other two are squared.
items.random_wrong: c^2 represents the side opposite the largest angle that is not 90 degrees.
items.why: Students treat the formula as symmetric in a, b, c, missing that c specifically denotes the hypotenuse.

items.q: Can Pythagoras' theorem be used on any triangle?
items.correct: No — it only applies to right-angled triangles.
items.misconception: Yes — it works on any triangle as long as you square the sides.
items.random_wrong: Only on equilateral triangles because all sides are equal.
items.why: Students learn the formula a^2 + b^2 = c^2 abstractly and forget the essential prerequisite — a 90 degree angle.

items.q: What is the relationship between the angle at the centre and the angle at the circumference subtended by the same arc?
items.correct: The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference.
items.misconception: The angles are equal because they are subtended by the same arc.
items.random_wrong: The angle at the centre is half the angle at the circumference.
items.why: Students intuitively expect 'same arc = same angle' and don't internalise that the central angle is double the inscribed angle.

items.q: In a² + b² = c², which side is c?
items.correct: The hypotenuse (longest, opposite the right angle)
items.why: It's The hypotenuse (longest, opposite the right angle). “The shortest side” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Pythagoras' theorem only works for triangles that have a:
items.why: Correct answer: right (90°) angle. “60° angle” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A right-angled triangle has short sides 5 cm and 12 cm. Find the hypotenuse, in cm.
items.why: Exact answer: 13. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
